raw.population: [Rambeck et al.] N:178 (Germany) Age: 29.5±15.2 (6-72) WT:54.5±16.7 [Chiba et al.] N: 104 (Japan) Age: 6.05±3.94 WT: 22.9±11.6 [Blain et al.] N: 40 (England) Age: 1.33±0.52 WT: 11.8±2.07
raw.objective: “we merged the data used to construct both nomograms with data obtained from Blain et al. (1981) and reanalysed them using a more general model than previously, and a data analysis approach [NONMEM (Sheiner et al., 1977)] specifically designed to provide accurate and precise estimates of population pharmacokinetic parameters from large numbers of individuals with only a small number of data points from each”
raw.covariates: Tested: AGE, WT, SEX, HT, RACE, data source Included: AGE, WT, RACE
raw.observations: The combined datasets were described using a simpler model than the original models combined, but it is difficult to determine if the description was as good. It would have been informative to provide the AIC of the complete complex model (describing each dataset separately using the separate models) and the AIC of the resulting model, thereby allowing the proper comparison and determine if the reduction in complexity versus loss in descriptive power was within reasonable limits (i.e. Occam’s razor).
raw.equations: SIZE=((WT/70)**theta2)*((HT/175) **0) Vm=theta1*SIZE*exp(eta1*(1-setB)+eta2*setB) Km=theta3*(theta4**RACE)*(theta5**AGE)*exp(eta3*(1-setB)+eta4*setB) DD=((Vm*Css)/(F*(Km+Css)))+eps1*(1-setB)+eps2*setB SIZE adjustment was estimated not to be significantly influenced by height, giving an exponent of 0. This reduces to: SIZE=((WT/70)**theta2). The full formula is given here for clarity. setB is dataset B (0=dataset A or C; 1= dataset B) RACE is race (0=European; 1=Japanese) AGE is age (above 15 years=0; under=1) The above formulation of Km was based on the description in the article. Given the reported estimates for Km in adult/paediatric Europeans and Japanese, the parameter values here do not perfectly correspond, i.e. Km in young Japanese is slight off (2.13 here vs 2.2 in the article). This is probably the result of some rounding being done in calculations in the article. The above parameterization (3 theta’s) should be preferred over separating each population (<15/>15; European/Japanese: 4 theta’s), resulting in less parameters to be estimated. The above parameterization may be further improved by making the difference between datasets in terms of eta’s and epsilons estimated using a proportional theta factor, e.g.: Km=…*exp(eta3*(1+setB*thetax)) where thetax is a fraction of increase (or decrease if <0) Given that theta’s are usually easier to estimate, this reduces complexity of the model while allowing it to describe the data to the same level of complexity. This assumes that no individuals occur in both datasets.
raw.parameter_values: theta1=415 theta2=0.6 theta3=5.7 theta4=0.67 theta5=0.56 omega1=0.0392 omega2=0.0120 omega3=0.223 omega4=0.273 sigma1=0.00737 sigma2=0.0143

extraction_target.notes: Appendix-derived silver reference; compare with the publication before promotion to a gold tier.

study.population: [Rambeck et al.] N:178 (Germany) Age: 29.5±15.2 (6-72) WT:54.5±16.7 [Chiba et al.] N: 104 (Japan) Age: 6.05±3.94 WT: 22.9±11.6 [Blain et al.] N: 40 (England) Age: 1.33±0.52 WT: 11.8±2.07

(1981) and reanalysed them using a more general model than previously, and a data analysis approach [NONMEM (Sheiner et al., 1977)] specifically designed to provide accurate and precise estimates of population pharmacokinetic parameters from large numbers of individuals with only a small number of data points from each”

covariates.raw: Tested: AGE, WT, SEX, HT, RACE, data source Included: AGE, WT, RACE

normalization.omega: Values labelled OMEGA in the appendix are retained as NONMEM variances; no CV reconversion is applied.
normalization.eta: ETA distribution is inferred only when the appendix equation explicitly uses exp(ETA) or additive ETA.
normalization.source_text: The appendix row is preserved verbatim in raw; normalized fields never replace it.
